ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — cooked fish dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (cooked fish dishes) is the closest defensible category for a miso-grilled sea bass set meal; no exact code exists for Japanese miso-grilled fish set meals in FoodEx2.

allergens: Gluten (miso paste, tempura batter, soy sauce)

serving.description: 1 set meal (grilled fish, rice, miso soup, side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion of approximately 600 g, comprising a grilled miso-marinated mero fillet, a bowl of steamed white rice, a bowl of miso soup, and assorted side dishes such as tempura.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimate for Japanese grilled fish set meal (Chilean sea bass grilled, white rice cooked, miso soup, tempura)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a complete miso-grilled mero set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component composition using USDA FoodData Central entries for: (1) Chilean sea bass, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159); (2) white rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); (3) miso soup (FDC ID 172453); (4) tempura batter, fried (FDC ID 168917). Input per_100g values (energy 125 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 5.83 g, carbohydrate 10.83 g) are broadly consistent with a mixed set meal of this composition and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g is plausible given miso paste and soy sauce content. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a direct entry for this dish. Values are flagged as estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a miso-grilled mero set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice, cooked, for which the University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 64–72 (mean ~65, medium category). The fish, miso soup, and tempura components contribute protein and fat that modestly attenuate the overall meal GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 64.98) / 100 ≈ 42.2 g, using 600 g serving × 10.83 g carbohydrate/100 g = 64.98 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from miso paste, soy sauce, and miso soup; estimated 300 mg sodium per 100 g equates to approximately 1,800 mg sodium per 600 g serving, approaching or exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium miso marinade, limit or omit soy sauce at the table, and avoid the miso soup or request a low-sodium broth substitute to keep the meal's sodium contribution below 600 mg per sitting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of approximately 65 (medium-high); the full 600 g serving delivers approximately 65 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~42, which is high and will produce a significant postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce the rice portion to half, request that mirin and sugar in the miso marinade be minimised, and avoid the tempura component to reduce refined-flour carbohydrate and glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Chilean sea bass (mero) provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein; however, the deep-fried tempura component contributes saturated fat, trans fat (0.08 g/100 g), and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are unfavourable for cardiovascular health, and the high sodium load is an additional cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should retain the grilled mero component for its omega-3 benefit, request no tempura or substitute with steamed vegetables, and ask for reduced-sodium miso marinade and broth to limit the combined sodium and saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried tempura generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which are a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis management; trace trans fat (0.08 g/100 g) from frying oil is also pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request that the tempura component be replaced with steamed or grilled vegetables; the grilled mero and miso components are beneficial due to omega-3 fatty acids and fermented food anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso Grilled Mero Set Meal is a Japanese-style set meal (teishoku) served in Singapore, featuring a fillet of Mero (Chilean Sea Bass, also known as Patagonian Toothfish) that has been marinated in a mixture of white miso paste, mirin, sake, and sugar, then grilled until caramelised and lightly charred on the surface. The fish is prized for its rich, buttery texture and mild, sweet flavour, which is enhanced by the umami depth of the miso marinade. The set is typically accompanied by a bowl of steamed white rice, a bowl of dashi-based miso soup with tofu and wakame seaweed, and side dishes that may include tempura (battered and deep-fried vegetables or prawns) or pickled vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 125 kcal per 100 g, with 7.5 g protein, 5.83 g fat (including 1.33 g saturated fat and a trace of trans fat at 0.08 g from the tempura frying oil), and 10.83 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg per 100 g due to miso paste and soy sauce, making a full 600 g serving deliver approximately 1,800 mg sodium. The glycemic index of the meal is estimated at 65 (medium) driven by the white rice component, with a glycemic load of approximately 42 per serving — clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The grilled mero component is an excellent source of omega-3 fatty acids and high-quality lean protein, beneficial for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory purposes, but the deep-fried tempura component introduces AGEs and additional saturated fat. This dish is not suitable for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. It is a popular lunch and dinner option at Japanese restaurants and food courts in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete miso-grilled mero set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component composition using USDA FoodData Central reference entries for Chilean sea bass (cooked, dry heat), white rice (cooked), miso soup, and tempura. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced record are broadly consistent with this composition and are retained. GI is estimated from the white rice GI anchor from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite miso-grilled mero set meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16; the dish is a multi-component set meal and no single approved-source entry covers the full fatty acid profile. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the input record and is not available from a single approved-source entry for this specific set meal combination. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite set meal are not available from a single approved-source entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. The input record carried two portions both flagged is_default=true (600 g and 650 g); the 600 g value is adopted as the canonical serving size as the more conservative estimate. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to a direct nutrient entry for this dish and is used only as a menu-listing reference; all nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central component estimates. Trans fat at 0.08 g/100 g is retained from the input record as a plausible trace value from tempura frying oil; this is below the 0.1 g/100 g labelling threshold in many jurisdictions. The dish is sold under the menu code 102D at the originating GrabFood restaurant.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full RESULT JSON from input. Identified that input source (foodo.sg URL) was not a direct nutrient entry; replaced with USDA FoodData Central component-estimation methodology.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 to align with allowed values. Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance and Q1/Q8 requirements for Singaporean dishes. Ensured all new aliases were added to the flat 'aliases' array.

Iteration 3: Fixed q8 failure — performed explicit aliases consistency self-check. Verified every string value in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases array. Confirmed Tamil alias corrected to exact match between aliases_by_language.tamil[0] ('மிசோ கிரில்ட் மெரோ உணவு தொகுப்பு') and flat aliases entry ('மிசோ கிரில்ட் மெரோ உணவு தொகுப்பு'). All 16 aliases_by_language string values confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases array.
